title: Prospective Predictors of Adolescent Screen Time and Problematic Screen Use.
abstract: OBJECTIVE: Adolescent digital media use is highly prevalent. However, potential harms are unclear, as prospective studies of outcomes of screen-naÃ¯ve youth are sparse. This study assessed whether individual differences in 4 domains relevant to addiction, measured in late childhood, prospectively predicted average daily hours of screen time (ST) and problematic screen use (PSU), defined as screen use that is compulsive and distressing, of 3 screen activities in mid-adolescence. METHOD: Youth (N = 4,754) with available data at the baseline and year 4 waves of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Developmentâ  (ABCD) Study (data release 5.1) were included. Independent linear mixed-effects regression models tested whether facets of impulsivity, motivation, psychopathology, and cognitive ability at ages 9 to 10, when screen use was minimal, predicted ST and PSU of video games, social media, and smartphones at ages 12 to 15, controlling for demographics and nesting participants within families and study sites. RESULTS: ST in mid-adolescence was positively predicted by baseline urgency, a facet of impulsivity, as well as reward sensitivity and externalizing. ST was negatively predicted by cognitive ability. Across all measured screen activities, PSU at ages 12 to 15 was positively predicted by urgency and punishment sensitivity, but not by cognitive abilities. PSU of video games and smartphones was predicted by externalizing. In mid-adolescence, ST and PSU of video games was predicted by internalizing. CONCLUSION: Engaging with digital media for many hours each day does not necessarily imply problematic use. Problematic screen use in mid-adolescence appears uniquely predicted by traits related to anxious distress. Comparisons with other addictive behaviors are discussed. CLINICAL GUIDANCE: â¢ Routinely assess the healthiness of young patients' screen use behaviors in mental health contexts.â¢ Initiate open-ended conversations with child and adolescent patients about their feelings about screen media.â¢ Encourage parents of adolescents to consider monitoring the screen use of their children, especially children with pre-existing mental health challenges. This study used personality, cognitive performance, mental health, and screen use data from thousands of young American adolescents tracked in their early teens as part of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Developmentâ  (ABCD) Study. The study found that 9- and 10-year-olds who report greater tendencies towards impulsivity when they feel strong emotions tend to report more problematic use of screen media (video games, social media, and mobile phones) four years later. Some traits that are known to increase peopleâs risk for drug addictions were related to problematic screen use, but others were not. This study suggests that young people who use screen media for a long time might not necessarily be âaddictedâ to their screens; rather, some young screen users who are already at risk for addictive behaviors might be more likely to develop a problem with screens.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Release 5.1 data from baseline and year 4 waves; N=4,754 youth with available data

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen time (average daily hours) and problematic screen use (PSU) for three screen activities: video games, social media, and smartphones

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects regression models testing whether baseline impulsivity, motivation, psychopathology, and cognitive ability predict screen time and PSU; multiple test statistics reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Results section reports associations with p-values and coefficient directions (e.g., 'ST in mid-adolescence was positively predicted by baseline urgency... and negatively predicted by cognitive ability'); specific numeric values in methods describe scales (e.g., PSU cutoffs)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis with linear mixed-effects models examining predictive associations from late childhood to mid-adolescence

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article in JAACAP Open, 2025, Volume 3, pages 1259-1269

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual psychological/clinical trait predictors (impulsivity facets [urgency, sensation-seeking, lack of planning, lack of perseverance], reward sensitivity, punishment sensitivity, externalizing, internalizing, cognitive ability). E16 applies: individual-characteristic predictors of phone/SM use MET regardless of direction/timeframe. Not a demographic-only FT-EC7 case.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (release 5.1), N=4,754 youth with baseline and year 4 data

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/SM variables: video game hours, social media hours, smartphones; also problematic screen use via VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire), MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire)

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports independent linear mixed-effects regression models testing whether baseline individual differences (impulsivity, motivation, psychopathology, cognitive ability) predict ST and PSU outcomes at ages 12-15

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 3 & 4 report β, SE, p-values for associations (e.g., video game hours: Urgency b=.07, SE=0.01, p<.001; social media hours: Urgency b=.06, SE=0.01, p<.001)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis; prospective design predicting screen time and problematic screen use at ages 12-15 from baseline measures at ages 9-10

C2.FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 applies: (a) Smartphone/SM variables (ST, PSU of video games, social media, smartphones) are the DEPENDENT variables in all reported analyses; (b) ALL predictors are individual psychological/behavioral traits (urgency, reward sensitivity, punishment sensitivity, externalizing, internalizing, cognitive ability) plus demographics (age, sex, race/ethnicity, income, parental education); (c) ALL analyses are LONGITUDINAL/PROSPECTIVE (baseline ages 9-10 predicting year 4 outcomes ages 12-15). No cross-sectional analyses, no SM as IV with non-SM outcome, no mediation/moderation involving SM.

title: The Pandemic's Toll on Young Adolescents: Prevention and Intervention Targets to Preserve Their Mental Health.
journal: The Journal of adolescent health : official publication of the Society for Adolescent Medicine
abstract: PURPOSE: Adolescence is characterized by dramatic physical, social, and emotional changes, making teens particularly vulnerable to the mental health effects of the COVID-19 pandemic. This longitudinal study identifies young adolescents who are most vulnerable to the psychological toll of the pandemic and provides insights to inform strategies to help adolescents cope better in times of crisis. METHODS: A data-driven approach was applied to a longitudinal, demographically diverse cohort of more than 3,000 young adolescents (11-14Â years) participating in the ongoing Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study in the United States, including multiple prepandemic visits and three assessments during the COVID-19 pandemic (May-August 2020). We fitted machine learning models and provided a comprehensive list of predictors of psychological distress in individuals. RESULTS: Positive affect, stress, anxiety, and depressive symptoms were accurately detected with our classifiers. Female sex and prepandemic internalizing symptoms and sleep problems were strong predictors of psychological distress. Parent- and youth-reported pandemic-related psychosocial factors, including poorer quality and functioning of family relationships, more screen time, and witnessing discrimination in relation to the pandemic further predicted youth distress. However, better social support, regular physical activities, coping strategies, and healthy behaviors predicted better emotional well-being. DISCUSSION: Findings highlight the importance of social connectedness and healthy behaviors, such as sleep and physical activity, as buffering factors against the deleterious effects of the pandemic on adolescents' mental health. They also point to the need for greater attention toward coping strategies that help the most vulnerable adolescents, particularly girls and those with prepandemic psychological problems.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses US ABCD Study data: 'Data were obtained from the US-based, multisite ABCD study (21 research sites from 17 states)...tracking >11,000 children, aged 9-10 years at baseline.' Longitudinal data from ABCD Release 3.0.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time as a measured variable: 'more screen time' is listed as a predictor of youth distress in the abstract and results. Figure descriptions and text mention 'screen time' as one of the pandemic-related psychosocial factors included in the machine learning models predicting psychological distress.

FT_IC3.evidence: Machine learning models (XGBoost, logistic regression) report feature importance via SHAP values, NOT traditional inferential test coefficients. Per E4 rule: 'ML — coefficient reported → MET. SHAP/Gini/permutation only → NOT_MET.' Paper states: 'Figure 2 shows the top 20 features by mean absolute SHAP value' and 'Feature importance of the GBT model.' No traditional stat test (t, F, p-value) specific to screen time association reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: No extractable numeric coefficient for screen time→outcome association. SHAP values are reported as feature importance (e.g., λ values shown in figures) but these are not traditional β, OR, r, p-values, or test statistics for a specific screen-time association. Screen time appears in feature importance rankings but without a discrete, extractable β/OR/p-value.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: 'We fitted machine learning models and provided a comprehensive list of predictors of psychological distress in individuals.' Longitudinal data with prepandemic baseline and pandemic-period outcomes (anxiety, depression, stress, positive affect).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2022. DOI: 10.1016/j.jadohealth.2021.11.023. No evidence of duplication or preprint status.

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Screen time is used as ONE predictor among many in a machine learning model predicting psychological distress (anxiety, depression, stress, positive affect). Screen time is NOT the dependent variable; it is an independent predictor. The model includes both demographic and individual psychological predictors (e.g., prepandemic internalizing symptoms, sleep problems, family relationships), and reports cross-sectional pandemic outcomes. Per E7: this is a traditional predictor→outcome direction.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data from more than 3,000 young adolescents (11-14 years) in the ongoing Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen time as a predictor: 'more screen time' identified as significant psychosocial factor predicting youth psychological distress during COVID-19 pandemic

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports machine learning model results predicting psychological distress; screen time included as one of multiple significant predictors in classifier models

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract mentions that screen time was a significant predictor but does not provide specific numeric coefficients, odds ratios, or p-values for the screen time predictor individually

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal study applying machine learning models to identify predictors of psychological distress in youth during pandemic

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in The Journal of Adolescent Health, 2022

C2.FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply because: (1) screen time is one predictor among many in a machine learning model; (2) paper includes individual psychological/behavioral predictors (anxiety, depressive symptoms, sleep problems, internalizing symptoms); (3) screen time→distress is tested alongside psychological factors, not just demographics

title: Adherence to 24-Hour Movement Recommendations and Health Indicators in Early Adolescence: Cross-Sectional and Longitudinal Associations in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study.
journal: The Journal of adolescent health : official publication of the Society for Adolescent Medicine
abstract: PURPOSE: Adherence to 24-hour movement guidelines of â¥60Â minutes of physical activity, â¤2 hours of screen time, and 9-11 hours of sleep has been shown to benefit cognitive, physical, and psychosocial health in children and young adolescents aged 5-13 years. However, these findings have mostly been based on cross-sectional studies or relatively small samples and the associations between adherence to guidelines and brain structure remain to be evaluated. METHODS: Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Developmentâ  (ABCD) study of 10,574 early adolescents aged 9-14 years from September 2016 to January 2021 were used to examine whether adherence to 24-hour movement guidelines benefits cognition (general cognitive ability, executive function, and learning/memory assessed by the National Institutes of Health Toolbox neurocognitive battery), body mass index, psychosocial health (internalizing, externalizing, and total problems from the parent-reported Child Behavior Checklist), and magnetic resonance imaging-derived brain morphometric measures at baseline (T1), â¼2Â years later (T2), and longitudinally from T1 to T2 (T2-T1). Multivariable linear mixed models were used, with adjustments for sociodemographic confounders. Time elapsed and T1 outcome measures were also controlled for in longitudinal models. RESULTS: Better cognitive scores, fewer behavioral problems, lower adiposity levels, and greater gray matter volumes were observed in those who met both sleep and screen time recommendations compared to those who met none. Longitudinal follow-up further supports these findings; participants who met both recommendations at T1 and T2 evidenced better outcome measures than those who met none. DISCUSSION: These findings support consideration of integrated rather than isolated movement recommendations across the day in early adolescence for better cognitive, physical and psychosocial health. Although the associations between physical activity and health indicators were less consistent in this study, the significant findings from sleep and screen time demonstrate the importance of considering movement recommendations in an integrated rather than isolated manner for adolescent health. It is recommended that movement behaviors be simultaneously targeted for better developmental outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study data with 10,574 early adolescents aged 9-14 years from September 2016 to January 2021

FT_IC2.evidence: Only generic '≤2 hours of screen time' from 24-hour movement guidelines composite; no specific smartphone or social media variable breakdown. Exclusion via FT-EC2: 'generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional and longitudinal linear mixed models examining adherence to 24-hour movement guidelines and health outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Adolescent Health 2023

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Does not meet full-text inclusion criteria (FT-IC2 failed); FT-EC7 evaluation not applicable

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data with 10,574 early adolescents at T1 and 9,273 at T2

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures weighted screen time (hours/day) as specific variable; also includes sleep and physical activity in 24-hour movement framework

FT_IC3.evidence: Multivariable linear mixed-effects models test associations between screen time and multiple non-SM outcomes (cognition, BMI, brain volumes, psychosocial health)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported in tables and text; β coefficients and p-values presented for screen time associations with outcomes

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis with both cross-sectional (T1) and longitudinal (T1→T2) designs using linear mixed models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2023; original peer-reviewed empirical work

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is used as independent variable (IV) predicting cognitive, physical, and behavioral outcomes, not as dependent variable. Analysis is cross-sectional and longitudinal predictive design.
